title: Epic: Unit Tests — Missing CLI modules (autopilot, doctor, init, monitor, providers)
description:
Five CLI modules have zero unit test coverage for their pure/stateless functions.
These all run without SSH or DB — straightforward to test with no infra overhead.

## Modules with missing pure-unit tests
- cli/autopilot.ts  — fetchBeadStats JSON parsing, resolveRemoteProjectPath, clearAndHeader tick logic
- cli/doctor.ts     — collectRecommendations() branch coverage per failure combo, render() column alignment
- cli/init.ts       — validateProjectName regex against good/bad inputs, makeAgentsMd content contract
- cli/monitor.ts    — renderFrame ETA math, agentStatusColor exhaustiveness + new default-branch
- cli/providers.ts  — maskKey() edge cases (key <= 8 chars, exactly 8, long), modelColor detection
- cli/index.ts      — parsePositiveInt() NaN / zero / negative / valid / float inputs

## Why no mocks needed
All target functions are pure: plain inputs → plain output or stdout text.
Use a real in-process call + stdout capture (console.log spy or process.stdout capture) where needed.

## Dependencies
Depends on: 6hs.5 (test runner setup), 5um.1 (Vitest install)

title: test/unit/autopilot-pure.test.ts — fetchBeadStats JSON parsing, resolveRemoteProjectPath
description:
Test the pure/stateless helpers in cli/autopilot.ts without SSH or DB.

## Functions to cover

### fetchBeadStats JSON parsing
- Input: br list --all --json output with various status combinations
- closed = issues where status === 'closed' (NOT tombstone — tombstones are cancelled)
- Verify tombstone issues do NOT inflate closed count
- Edge cases: empty array, all open, all closed, mixed, invalid JSON → null

### resolveRemoteProjectPath
- With remoteRepoRoot and projectName → correct joined path
- Trailing slash on remoteRepoRoot is stripped
- No projectName → undefined returned

### clearAndHeader tick logic
- tick === 1: no screen clear, emits blank line instead
- tick > 1: emits ANSI clear sequence (\x1b[2J\x1b[H)

## Test file
test/unit/autopilot-pure.test.ts

## No mocks needed
Import the module-level pure functions directly; capture stdout for header checks.

## Depends on
6hs.5 (test runner setup)

title: Epic: JSON Output Contract Tests — machine-readable command shapes
description:
Every flywheel command that produces JSON output must have a schema contract test.
The JSON shapes are API contracts — downstream tools (CI scripts, dashboards, autopilot) depend on them.
Breaking a JSON shape silently is worse than a test failure.

## Commands with JSON output
- flywheel runs (--format json): { run: FlywheelRun, events: PhaseEvent[] } per run
- flywheel replay <id> --format json: { run: FlywheelRun, events: PhaseEvent[] }
- flywheel beads triage: bv --robot-triage --format json output shape
- FlywheelServer /snapshot: DashboardSnapshot shape
- FlywheelServer /action response: { ok, action, payload } envelope
- NTM bridge: ntm list --json, ntm status --json shapes

## What to test
For each: seed data, run command, parse JSON, assert every required field exists and has correct type.
Use JSON Schema or explicit field assertions — no snapshot strings (they break on minor formatting changes).

## Dependencies
Depends on: 6hs.5, 5um.2 (test helpers), 3b5.4 (state unit tests)

title: test/contract/flywheel-runs-json.test.ts — replay --format json schema
description:
Contract test for flywheel replay <id> --format json output shape.

## Setup
Seed a flywheel_run with phase_events, bead_snapshots, and api_calls in a temp DB.
Run: flywheel replay <id> --format json (using FLYWHEEL_HOME pointed at temp dir).

## Schema assertions
Top level: { run: object, events: array }
run fields: id (string UUID), project_name (string), phase (plan|beads|swarm|review|deploy),
  started_at (ISO 8601), completed_at (ISO 8601 | null), checkpoint_sha (string | null),
  cost_usd (number | null), notes (string | null)
events[*] fields: id (number), run_id (string), event_type (string), timestamp (ISO 8601),
  phase_from (string | null), phase_to (string | null), actor (string | null), payload_json (string | null)

## Edge cases
- Run with zero events: { run: {...}, events: [] }
- Run with 100+ events: all emitted (no truncation)
- payload_json field: must be valid JSON string or null (not double-encoded)
- --since 1h applied to a run with old events: events array is empty, run is still present

## Also test: exit 0 on success, exit 1 on unknown run ID, 'Run not found' message on stderr

title: test/unit/ubs-false-positive-audit.test.ts — document and verify all UBS false positives
description:
A code-level test that verifies each UBS false positive documented in .ubsignore is genuinely a false positive and hasn't accidentally become a real issue.

## What to verify

1. 'Switch cases missing break' — scan all switch statements in cli/*.ts; assert EVERY case branch ends with return/throw/break (no unintentional fall-through)

2. 'Function declarations in blocks' — scan all cli/*.ts for function declarations inside if/else/try/catch blocks; assert ZERO found

3. 'Nested ternary operators' — the flagged lines are TypeScript type annotations; assert the flagged line numbers do NOT contain ternary operators (? : not preceded by type annotation context)

4. 'Blocking dialogs' — the prompt() identifier in rollback.ts is a local async function (readline-based, not window.prompt); assert it is not called from within a synchronous context

5. 'Synchronous fs.*Sync calls' — assert each sync call is in a startup/init context (not inside an async function that is called during normal command execution with live SSH connections)

6. 'any[] in SSHStreamChannel' — assert the two any[] usages have the eslint-disable comment and are only in the private SSHStreamChannel interface, not exported

## Why this matters
.ubsignore documents decisions. This test ensures the decisions remain valid after code changes. If someone removes the eslint-disable comment or moves a function into a live-request handler, this test will catch it.

## Depends on
6hs.5 (test runner setup)

title: test/integration/budget-enforcement.test.ts — --budget cap stops swarm before spawn
description:
Verify that the --budget flag on flywheel swarm enforces the spending cap correctly.

## Scenarios

### Pre-existing spend exceeds budget (resume scenario)
1. Create a flywheel_run with existing api_calls totalling $5.00
2. Run flywheel swarm 3 --budget 4.00 (budget < existing spend)
3. Assert: exit non-zero; error mentions 'Budget cap exceeded'; NO ntm spawn called;
   state DB does NOT have a new swarm_spawned event

### New run (budget > 0 spend — always passes pre-flight)
1. Run flywheel swarm 3 --budget 100.00 with a fresh run
2. Pre-flight budget check passes (cost = 0 < 100)
3. Note: post-spawn enforcement is handled by status() in the monitoring loop —
   NOT tested here (status() tested in unit tests)

### No --budget flag (no cap)
1. Run flywheel swarm 3 without --budget
2. Assert: budgetUsd is undefined in the StartSwarmResult (no enforcement at all)

### Invalid --budget value
1. flywheel swarm 3 --budget foo → error about invalid number, exit 1
2. flywheel swarm 3 --budget -1 → error about positive value, exit 1

## Test environment
Use a mock NTM spawn (don't need a real VPS for pre-flight budget check tests).
For scenario 1, can test entirely with in-memory state using FLYWHEEL_HOME temp dir.

## Dependencies
Depends on: 3b5.4 (state unit tests), 6hs.2 (temp-dir fixture), cvw.8 (FLYWHEEL_HOME)

title: test/unit/security-no-secret-leak.test.ts — API keys never appear in CLI output
description:
Security regression test: verify that no CLI command leaks raw API keys or private key contents to stdout/stderr.

## What to test
1. flywheel providers — output contains masked keys (sk-ant-api0…N4e5 format), NOT full keys
   - maskKey('sk-ant-api03-abc123') → 'sk-ant-ap…123' (first 8 + last 4)
   - maskKey('short') → '****'
   - Full key MUST NOT appear anywhere in the output string

2. flywheel doctor — ssh.yaml check prints 'loaded + key file readable', NOT the key path's contents

3. flywheel settings ssh — re-running reads existing config and shows defaults but NEVER reveals the full key on screen (the @inquirer/prompts default value is shown as a mask)

4. wizard.ts fan-out error output — if Anthropic call fails, the error message MUST NOT include the apiKey from providers.yaml

## How to implement
For each: write to a temp providers.yaml with a known 'fake-but-real-format' key. Run the command. Capture stdout+stderr as a string. Assert the full fake key string does NOT appear anywhere in the combined output. Assert the masked form (first 8 + ... + last 4) DOES appear.

## Depends on
6hs.2 (temp-dir fixture), 6hs.5 (runner setup)

title: test/integration/detailed-logging-harness.ts — structured test transcript with timestamps
description:
Implement the detailed logging harness required by cvw.11.3 logging policy.

## What it does
Wraps every test command invocation and logs:
- ISO timestamp (start + end)
- Full command + arguments (sanitized — no raw API keys)
- Working directory
- Environment variables used (names only, not values)
- stdout (full)
- stderr (full)
- Exit code
- Wall time (ms)
- Memory delta (optional: process.memoryUsage before/after)

## Output format
JSONL file: one JSON object per command invocation, newline-delimited.
Human-readable text log alongside for quick inspection.
On test failure: dump the last N command logs to test output automatically.

## Artifact directory
test/artifacts/YYYY-MM-DD-HHMMSS-<testSuiteName>/
  - transcript.jsonl    (machine-readable)
  - transcript.txt      (human-readable)
  - state-db-snapshot.sqlite  (copy of state.db at test end)
  - files/              (any temp files created during test)

## API
loggedExec(cmd: string, args: string[], opts?: ExecOptions): Promise<LoggedResult>
loggedSpawn(cmd: string, args: string[]): ChildProcess  (for long-running commands)

## Integration
The harness is a helper module imported by all integration and E2E tests.
It has no test assertions itself — it is infrastructure only.

## Dependencies
Depends on: 6hs.3 (process output capture), 6hs.5 (test runner setup)

title: test/e2e/lifecycle/01-plan-to-deploy.e2e.ts — full wizard→beads→swarm→review→deploy
description:
The crown-jewel E2E test: drive every flywheel phase end-to-end against a real VPS.

## Sequence
1. flywheel settings ssh (non-interactive: write config programmatically)
2. flywheel ssh test — assert exit 0, latency logged to state DB
3. flywheel preflight — assert all required tools present
4. flywheel new "test-project-e2e" --fast — assert plan.md created, wizard_run in DB marked completed
5. flywheel beads generate — assert br list shows beads exist on VPS
6. flywheel beads triage --top 3 — assert JSON output with at least 1 recommendation
7. flywheel swarm 1 -- (single agent, no commit) — assert session created, checkpoint SHA recorded in DB
8. flywheel gate advance --sha $(git -C ... rev-parse HEAD) — assert flywheel_run.phase advances
9. flywheel review --passes fresh-review -- assert prompts dispatched to pane
10. flywheel deploy — inject DEPLOY test-project-e2e confirmation, assert deploy_completed event

## Logging
Emit TIMESTAMP CMD ARGS EXIT_CODE WALL_TIME for each step.
On any failure: dump stdout/stderr and the last 50 phase_events from state DB.
Write artifacts to test/e2e/artifacts/<runId>/.

## Assertions
- Phase gate transitions are in correct order
- Checkpoint SHA in state DB matches remote HEAD before swarm
- plan.md contains expected sections (# Plan, ## Adversarial Risk Assessment)
- All phase_events are present and in chronological order
- Total cost is non-negative and recorded in api_calls table
- Deploy event shows afterSha differs from beforeSha if commit was needed

title: test/e2e/lifecycle/02-rollback-verification.e2e.ts — checkpoint SHA + git reset
description:
Verify the rollback flow is safe and correct.

## Sequence
1. Run flywheel swarm to create a checkpoint SHA in state DB
2. Make a test commit on VPS (touch a dummy file, git add + commit)
3. Verify git log on VPS shows the new commit ahead of checkpoint
4. Run flywheel rollback (with programmatic ROLLBACK confirmation)
5. Verify VPS git HEAD now matches the checkpoint SHA
6. Verify rollback event logged in phase_events with correct SHA
7. Verify the dummy file no longer exists on VPS

## Safety assertions
- Rollback rejects non-hex SHA input (assertSafeSha check)
- Rollback prompts for ROLLBACK confirmation and cancels on wrong input
- SSH is disconnected cleanly after rollback (no leaked connection)
- state DB records rollback event before asserting success

## Cleanup
Restore VPS to original HEAD after test (git cherry-pick or re-commit the dummy change)

title: test/e2e/error-matrix/01-ssh-and-config-failures.e2e.ts — every config+SSH error mode

Exhaustive test of every config-missing and SSH-error scenario.

title: test/e2e/concurrent/01-dual-process-state.e2e.ts — two CLI processes, same SQLite DB
description:
Verify that two simultaneous CLI processes do not corrupt the SQLite state DB.

## Why this matters
better-sqlite3 is synchronous. Two Node.js processes sharing the same state.db
must not produce 'database is locked' errors or corrupted rows. WAL mode
(PRAGMA journal_mode = WAL) is already set in initDb() — test that it works.

## Test sequence
1. Spawn Process A: flywheel gate status (fast read-only query)
2. Immediately spawn Process B: flywheel runs (another read-only query)
3. Both should complete successfully with exit 0
4. Spawn Process A: flywheel gate advance (write operation)
5. Simultaneously spawn Process B: flywheel runs (read while write in progress)
6. Both should complete; no 'database is busy' errors
7. Spawn 5 concurrent processes all doing flywheel runs — all must exit 0

## Assertions
- No process exits with 'DATABASE_ERROR' or 'database is busy'
- State DB is not corrupted after all processes finish (verify with flywheel doctor)
- Concurrent reads never block each other (WAL mode guarantee)
- Write followed by immediate read from different process sees committed data

## Important
Run this test last in the E2E suite — it exercises the same DB as other tests.
Use a dedicated test DB path (/concurrent-test/state.db) to isolate.

title: Epic: E2E Local-only CLI Tests — real binary, no VPS required
description:
Tests that spawn node dist/cli.js as a real subprocess and assert on exit codes plus stdout/stderr — WITHOUT needing a VPS or SSH config. These cover commands that only touch local state (sqlite, config files, prompts.yaml).

Key difference from unit tests: unit tests import TypeScript modules directly; these run the actual compiled binary end-to-end. Catches wiring bugs, commander.js config errors, process.exit issues.

Must run after npm run build since they invoke dist/cli.js directly.
Test isolation: each test overrides HOME env var to a unique temp dir.

title: test/e2e/local-commands.e2e.ts — real binary, HOME-isolated, no VPS
description:

Must run after npm run build. Spawns node dist/cli.js as a subprocess.
Each test overrides HOME via process.env.HOME + FLYWHEEL_HOME to a unique temp dir.

Helper: async function runFlywheel(args, opts?): Promise<{exitCode, stdout, stderr, durationMs}>

 - captures all output, times the invocation
 - logs [E2E] cmd, [E2E] exit:N in Xms, [E2E] stdout:..., [E2E] stderr:...

TESTS:

flywheel gate status:

flywheel gate advance:

title: Epic: Unit Tests — Local HTTP and WebSocket server
description:
Start a real FlywheelServer on a random OS-assigned port (port:0) and make real HTTP/WebSocket connections to it. No mocking of the server internals. Tests that SSHManager/NtmBridge connections fail gracefully when VPS is unreachable are still valid because the server handles errors without crashing.

Covers: GET /health (200 + generatedAt), GET /snapshot (returns snapshot shape), POST /action with malformed JSON (400), POST /action body > 256KB (rejected), POST /action unknown type (error response), WebSocket connect receives initial snapshot, WebSocket send action receives action_result, server.stop() closes all connections, resolveDashboardAsset returns 404 for missing assets.

Depends on: test-infrastructure

title: test/unit/server-http.test.ts — GET /health, /snapshot, 404; body size limit
description:
File: test/unit/server-http.test.ts
Start a real FlywheelServer on port 0 (OS-assigned). Extract the actual port from server.httpServer.address(). Use Node.js built-in fetch or undici for requests.

Setup:
  let server: FlywheelServer;
  let baseUrl: string;
  beforeEach: server = createFlywheelServer({ port: 0 }); await server.start(); baseUrl = ...
  afterEach: await server.stop();

GET /health:
- status 200
- body.ok === true
- body.generatedAt is valid ISO string

GET /snapshot:
- status 200
- body has keys: generatedAt, server, ssh, agents, beads, actions
- body.ssh.connected is boolean
- body.agents is array

GET /unknown-path:
- status 404
- body.ok === false

POST /action with malformed JSON body (e.g. '{bad'):
- status 400
- body.ok === false
- body.error mentions 'JSON'

POST /action body larger than 256KB:
- status 500 (readRequestBody throws)
- body.ok === false
- body.error mentions 'limit'

POST /action with valid JSON but unknown type { type: 'nonexistent' }:
- status 200 (handleAction falls through, returns undefined)
- OR status 400 if server now validates action types
- either way: no server crash, response received

Acceptance: server starts, handles requests, stops cleanly without uncaught exceptions

title: Epic: Vite Dashboard (Monitoring Glass + Action Surface)
description:
A locally-served web UI (localhost:4200) providing live view of flywheel state with a narrow, controlled action surface. Connects to CLI server over local WebSocket — never directly to VPS.

## Architecture (Critical)
Browser (dashboard) ←→ WebSocket ←→ flywheel CLI server (localhost) ←→ SSH ←→ VPS

The WS connection is LOCAL. The server polls SSH on 15-30s schedule and pushes deltas. This topology is non-negotiable — do not build a push layer from VPS to dashboard directly.

## Panels
- PipelineView — phase progress + gate controls
- BeadBoard — kanban with PageRank scores + critical path + velocity ETA
- AgentPanel — per-agent status, Agent Mail traffic, file locks
- LogStream — multiplexed ntm activity + Agent Mail + optional app logs
- PromptLibrary — browse + send prompts to agents
- MemoryPanel — cass/cm session memory browser
- ProviderSettings — CAAM slot status

## Mutation Boundary (3 permitted actions ONLY)
1. Prompt injection (delivers named prompt to agent(s))
2. Swarm pause/resume (sends ntm pause/resume)
3. Gate advance (approves current gate, unblocks next phase)

The dashboard may APPROVE what's in motion; it may never INITIATE new phases. 'Start Swarm' and 'Deploy' are CLI-only. This is a hard line.

## Fallback
If weeks 1-4 slip, dashboard-less ship with flywheel monitor (terminal mode) is the named fallback. CLI-only with terminal monitoring covers the gap.

title: test/unit/gate-integration.test.ts — gateStatus, gateAdvance with real StateManager

SQLite database. This is distinct from 2eo.4 (which only tests pure phase logic
like nextPhase() and assertSafeSha()). These tests verify the DB-backed gate
state machine that powers the human workflow gate.

Setup: const db = initDb(':memory:'); const sm = new StateManager(db);
Use a spy/capture on console.log output to verify printed text (Vitest vi.spyOn).

gateStatus() with no runs:
- prints 'No flywheel runs found'
- does not throw
- exits with code 0 (check process.exitCode, not process.exit)

gateStatus() with a run in 'plan' phase, gate not passed:
- prints the run ID (8-char prefix)
- prints 'Phase: plan'
- prints 'Gate: waiting'
- prints 'Next: beads'
- prints 'Advance: flywheel gate advance'

gateStatus() with gate already passed:
- prints 'Gate: ✓ passed' with timestamp

gateAdvance() with no runs:
- throws or calls process.exit(1)
- error message mentions 'No flywheel runs found'

gateAdvance() valid — plan → beads:
- calls advanceGate on StateManager
- DB: run.phase === 'beads'
- DB: run.gate_passed_at is not null
- prints '✓ Gate advanced: plan → beads'

gateAdvance() with --sha option:
- DB: run.checkpoint_sha === provided SHA
- SHA printed in output (12-char prefix)

gateAdvance() on 'deploy' phase:
- prints 'terminal phase' message
- does NOT throw, exits 0

gateAdvance() gate notification hook (FLYWHEEL_GATE_HOOK):
- set process.env.FLYWHEEL_GATE_HOOK = 'echo HOOK_FIRED'
- call gateAdvance() on a valid run
- hook output captured in subprocess (verify via spawnSync or real subprocess)
- hook receives FLYWHEEL_GATE_FROM and FLYWHEEL_GATE_TO env vars
- hook failure (non-zero exit) prints warning but does NOT abort gateAdvance

FLYWHEEL_GATE_HOOK_TIMEOUT_MS:
- set to '100', hook is 'sleep 10' → hook times out after 100ms
- warning printed, gateAdvance still completes

Acceptance: full state machine verified — no run, waiting, passed, terminal,
hook success, hook timeout, hook failure all covered

description:

- optional fields undefined when not provided
- cause chain: err.cause === provided cause Error

Error classification observable behavior — test by running runRemote against a
real spawned local command (use child_process.spawn + streams on localhost without SSH):
OR simply verify RemoteCommandError is thrown for non-zero exits:
- Construct an SSH mock using a real Unix socket or local port (no VPS needed)

Indirect tests via RemoteCommandRunner against real local execution:
- A command that exits non-zero ('false' or 'exit 1') →

    err.exitCode matches the exit code
    err.command contains the command string
- 'permission denied' in stderr → code becomes PERMISSION_DENIED
  (run: 'cat /root/secret' or 'chmod 000 /tmp/test && cat /tmp/test')

Acceptance: RemoteCommandError is-a Error, all error codes accessible,
optional fields optional — guards against accidental breaking of error handling chain

title: Epic: Prompt Engine (The Playbook)
description:
Maintains the full 25+ prompt library as versioned YAML config, routes prompts to the right model/effort tier, and enables injection from CLI or dashboard to specific agents.

## Why It Matters
The flywheel workflow is defined by its prompts. Without a prompt engine, prompts live in docs and get copy-pasted manually. With one, they become addressable, versionable, injectable, and context-aware (via dynamic variables).

## Core Capability
- Load prompts from prompts.yaml with model/effort/phase metadata
- Dynamic variable substitution at send time ({plan_path}, {bead_count}, {open_bead_count}, etc.)
- CLI: flywheel prompts list, flywheel prompts send
- Route to specific agents or broadcast to all

## Shaped Boundary
The prompt engine is a config reader + router. It does NOT generate prompts, modify them, or decide when to send them. That's the human's job (or autopilot's job in --autopilot mode).

title: Epic: Phase 1 Planning Wizard (Highest-Leverage Piece)
description:
The Planning Wizard is the single highest-leverage component in the console. It takes a raw idea and produces a synthesis-grade planning packet through automated multi-model conversation.

## Why Highest-Leverage
Every agent-hour in Phase 3 (swarm) is a bet on the plan being right. The wizard makes that bet dramatically better by:
1. Getting competing perspectives from 3 different models (parallel fan-out)
2. Synthesizing them into one coherent plan (Opus)
3. Stress-testing the plan adversarially before any code is written
4. Generating brilliant enhancement ideas (3 rounds)

## The Workflow
User types idea → PARALLEL FAN-OUT (Opus + GPT-4o + Gemini) → Synthesis Pass 1 → Synthesis Pass 2 → Adversarial Challenge → Brilliant Ideas ×3 → GATE → Final packet (plan.md + wizard-log.jsonl)

## Key UX Decision
Show parallel fan-out in real-time — each model's response streams into a labeled column as it arrives. Watching it work is part of the magic.

## Error Policy (from pitch rabbit holes)
If one of three models fails mid-wizard: save the two successful plans, fail loudly, allow resume from synthesis step. Do NOT silently skip — synthesis quality degrades with incomplete inputs.

## Shippable Standalone
The wizard is useful even without the rest of the console. It could be shipped as its own tool.

title: Epic: Top 10 Enhancements (v1)
description:
Ten enhancements that emerged from a structured 100-idea longlist, scored against leverage per implementation dollar, experience transformation, and recurring pain. All labeled v1 — they fit in the 6-week appetite.

1. Stuck Agent Auto-Nudge — self-healing swarm
2. Adversarial Challenge Pass — stress-test plans before coding
3. Velocity-Based ETA — confident delegation with live projections
4. flywheel doctor — 10-second full diagnostics
5. Dynamic Prompt Variables — context-aware prompts for free
6. Pre-Swarm Git Checkpoint + rollback — bounded downside
7. Gate Notification Hooks — start and walk away
8. flywheel replay — audit log as learning tool
9. Bead State Local Backup — foundational data layer
10. Per-Phase Cost Tracking — close the model-selection feedback loop

Most of these are integrated into their parent components. This epic tracks the ones that are cross-cutting or standalone.

title: Epic: Swarm Orchestration + Review Pipeline + Deploy
description:
The execution phases of the flywheel — spawning agents, managing the commit agent, running review passes, and deploying. This is where the console orchestrates NTM (Core Principle #2: we orchestrate NTM, not replace it).

## Swarm (Phase 3)
- flywheel swarm N: spawns N agents via ntm spawn, includes commit agent on 15-20min timer
- Git checkpoint before spawn (SHA stored for rollback)
- CAAM slot rotation (round-robin at spawn time)
- Stuck agent auto-nudge (idle >8min → configurable prompt injection)
- Budget cap (--budget flag, hard-stop on threshold)

## Review (Phase 4) — 8 Named Passes
1. fresh-review: self-review with fresh eyes
2. peer-review: review code from fellow agents
3. ui-ux-scrutiny: Stripe-level polish
4. ubs-scan: run UBS, fix findings
5. test-coverage: unit + e2e audit, create test beads if gaps
6. orm-audit: schema/model review
7. tanstack-optimize: TanStack library opportunities
8. dcg-safety: verify DCG hook

## Deploy (Phase 5)
- Final commit + gh flow + checksums + CM reflect
- Requires typing exact DEPLOY <project-name> (case-sensitive)

## Key Principle
No human code edits once agents are running (Core Principle #5). The human's job is observation and gate decisions.

title: Epic: SSH Layer (Component 1 — Foundation)
description:
The SSH layer is the foundation that every other component depends on. All agent execution, monitoring, and control flows through a single persistent SSH connection to the ACFS VPS.

## Why This Is First
Nothing works without SSH connectivity. The console is a cockpit that orchestrates remote execution — the SSH layer is the wire between cockpit and engine. Every subsequent component (wizard, prompt engine, dashboard) depends on being able to run remote commands reliably.

## What It Includes
- Interactive SSH config setup (ssh.yaml)
- Connection testing with latency measurement
- Preflight tool verification on VPS
- Persistent node-ssh connection management
- Remote command runner with timeout, exit-code capture, stderr/stdout split
- Log streaming (tail -f over SSH)

## Architecture Decision
The console never runs agents locally. All execution happens on the VPS via SSH. This is Core Principle #1 and is non-negotiable. The SSH layer enforces this by being the ONLY way the console touches the VPS.

## Key Rabbit Hole (from pitch)
Do NOT build SSH reconnection/session recovery in v1. The VPS keeps running autonomously whether or not the SSH session is alive. Fail loudly with a red indicator; user reconnects manually. This is fine because ntm/agents are resilient to SSH drops.

title: Persistent SSH connection manager (node-ssh)
description:
## What
Implement the core SSH connection manager using node-ssh. Maintains ONE persistent connection reused across all commands. Exposes connect(), disconnect(), exec(), and stream() methods.

## Why
This is the wire between the cockpit (console) and the engine (VPS). Every remote operation flows through this single connection. It must be reliable, handle timeouts, and split stdout/stderr properly.

## Implementation
- ssh.ts module exporting SSHManager class
- connect(): reads ssh.yaml, establishes connection, stores instance
- disconnect(): clean teardown
- exec(command, opts?): runs command, returns { stdout, stderr, exitCode }
  - opts.timeout: configurable per-command timeout (default: 30s)
  - opts.stream: if true, pipes stdout to provided writable stream
- stream(command): returns a readable stream of command output (for tail -f)
- isConnected(): check connection state
- getLatency(): measures round-trip with echo command

## Error Handling
- Connection failure: throw with clear message including host + port
- Command timeout: kill remote process, throw timeout error
- Connection drop mid-command: throw, set isConnected = false
- DO NOT auto-reconnect in v1 (explicit rabbit hole from pitch)

## Testing
- Unit tests with mocked node-ssh
- Manual integration test: connect to real VPS, run echo, verify output

## Dependencies
- Depends on: SSH config persistence (needs ssh.yaml to read)

## Key Design Decision
Single persistent connection, not connection-per-command. This avoids SSH handshake overhead on every operation (adds ~200-500ms per command otherwise).

## Implementation

- Output: green ✓ with latency on success, red ✗ with error on failure

- Bad host → red error with clear message
- Bad key → red error mentioning key path
- Connection logged to SQLite

title: flywheel preflight — VPS tool verification
description:
## What
Verify that all required tools exist on the VPS: ntm, br, bv, gh, git, agent-mail, ubs, dcg. Hard-fails by default; --force warns and continues.

## Why
The console orchestrates VPS tools — if they're missing, everything downstream fails in confusing ways. Preflight catches this upfront.

## Implementation
- For each tool: run `which <tool>` and optionally `<tool> --version` via SSH
- Required tools: ntm, br, bv, gh, git
- Recommended tools: agent-mail, ubs, dcg, cass, cm
- Exit code contract:
  - 0: all required checks passed
  - 2: warnings present, continued with --force
  - 3: required check failed, hard stop
- Color-coded output: green ✓ for found, red ✗ for missing required, yellow ⚠ for missing recommended
- --force flag: downgrade hard failures to warnings (exit 2 instead of 3)

## Acceptance
- `flywheel preflight` on a properly configured VPS → all green, exit 0
- Missing required tool → red ✗, exit 3

title: Remote command runner with timeout + streaming
description:
## What
Higher-level wrapper around SSHManager.exec() that adds structured output capture, timeout handling, and streaming support for long-running commands like tail -f.

## Why
Many console operations need to stream output (log tailing, wizard fan-out progress), handle timeouts gracefully (agent monitoring), and capture exit codes reliably (preflight, deploy). This is the reusable building block.

## Implementation
- runRemote(cmd, opts): structured wrapper
  - Returns: { stdout, stderr, exitCode, duration }
  - opts.timeout: per-command timeout (throws on exceed)
  - opts.onStdout: callback for streaming stdout chunks
  - opts.onStderr: callback for streaming stderr chunks
  - opts.silent: suppress output (for background commands)
- streamRemote(cmd): returns AsyncIterable<string> for line-by-line streaming
  - Used for `tail -f` log streaming, ntm activity polling
  - Supports cancellation via AbortController
- Error classification: TIMEOUT, CONNECTION_LOST, COMMAND_FAILED, PERMISSION_DENIED

## Acceptance
- runRemote('echo hello') returns { stdout: 'hello', exitCode: 0 }
- runRemote with 1s timeout on sleep 10 throws TIMEOUT
- streamRemote on tail -f yields lines as they appear
- Cancellation via AbortController stops the stream

## Dependencies
- Depends on: SSH connection manager

comments.author: RedStream
comments.text: Implemented `cli/remote.ts` as a higher-level wrapper over `SSHManager`: `runRemote()` returns structured output/duration, `streamRemote()` exposes line-wise async iteration with `AbortSignal`, and failures are classified as TIMEOUT / CONNECTION_LOST / COMMAND_FAILED / PERMISSION_DENIED. Validation: `npm run typecheck`, `npm run build`.

title: NTM bridge — ntm commands over SSH
description:
## What
Typed wrapper around ntm commands executed over SSH. Provides structured interfaces for ntm spawn, ntm send, ntm activity, ntm pause, ntm resume, ntm list.

## Why
Core Principle #2: NTM is the execution layer; we orchestrate NTM, not replace it. The NTM bridge is how the console talks to the agent execution plane. Every swarm, review, and monitoring operation goes through this.

## Implementation
- ntm-bridge.ts module
- spawn(project, count, opts?): runs `ntm spawn <project> <count>`, returns pane IDs
- send(project, pane, message): runs `ntm send <project> <pane> "<message>"`
- activity(project): runs `ntm activity <project>`, returns parsed agent status
- pause(project): runs `ntm pause <project>`
- resume(project): runs `ntm resume <project>`
- list(): runs `ntm list`, returns active sessions
- Types: NtmSession, NtmPane, NtmActivity

## Parsing Considerations
ntm output is human-readable, not designed for machine parsing. Wrap parsing in a thin adapter layer with tests (per pitch rabbit hole #3 about br stats parsing — same principle applies to ntm output). If ntm adds --json output later, only the adapter changes.

## Acceptance
- ntmBridge.list() returns typed session list
- ntmBridge.spawn('project', 4) creates 4 panes
- ntmBridge.activity('project') returns per-agent status
- Parser handles ntm output format changes gracefully (adapter pattern)

## Dependencies
- Depends on: Remote command runner

comments.author: RedStream
comments.text: Implemented `cli/ntm-bridge.ts` on top of `RemoteCommandRunner`. It uses installed NTM JSON commands (`ntm list --json`, `ntm status <session> --json`, `ntm send <session> --pane=N --json`, `ntm interrupt <session> --json`) and keeps idle/stuck detection state across polls. Important note: the installed NTM 1.2.0 does not expose a dedicated `activity` or `resume` command, so `activity()` is derived from `status --json` and `resume()` fails loudly with an explicit unsupported-command error instead of faking behavior.

title: Epic: Test Coverage — unit, integration, and e2e
description:
Full testing pyramid for agent-flywheel-console.

## Context
Zero tests exist. No test runner. No test scripts. Every module has been built and shipped without automated verification.

## Strategy

No mocks, no fake SSH connections, no stub DBs. Every test must exercise the real implementation.

title: Test infrastructure: runner, helpers, fixtures
description:
Everything needed before any test can be written.

## What
1. node:test runner wired into package.json (no new runtime dep — node:test ships with Node 20)
2. tsx test execution: `node --import tsx/esm --test 'test/**/*.test.ts'`
3. Shared test helpers in test/helpers/:
   - tmpDir() — creates + auto-cleans a temp directory per test
   - makeTempSshConfig(opts) — writes a real ssh.yaml to a temp path
   - makeMemoryDb() — opens a :memory: better-sqlite3 DB and runs full SCHEMA
   - seedFlywheelRun(db, overrides) — inserts a flywheel_run row, returns id
   - seedPhaseEvents(db, runId, events) — inserts phase_events for replay tests
   - seedBeadSnapshots(db, runId, snapshots[]) — inserts bead_snapshots for velocity tests
4. Test fixtures in test/fixtures/:
   - ssh.yaml — valid SSH config pointing at 127.0.0.1:9999 (connection tests use error path)
   - providers.yaml — valid multi-slot config with fake keys sk-ant-test-...
   - prompts-minimal.yaml — minimal prompts.yaml with 3 prompts for prompt engine tests

## Acceptance
- `npm test` runs all tests and exits 0 when all pass
- Individual test files can be run: `node --import tsx/esm --test test/unit/utils.test.ts`
- Helpers are well-typed TypeScript with JSDoc
- No test imports anything from outside test/ except the module under test

HTTP GET /unknown-path

## Why this is missing today
The current test tree covers pure helpers, config loaders, state, server, and real-VPS E2E. It does NOT yet cover the commander.js command surface itself: argument validation, exit codes, stdout/stderr contracts, and local state-driven terminal behavior.

title: test/unit/dashboard.test.mjs — escapeHtml, escapeAttribute, formatters
description:
## What
Test pure helper functions in dashboard/main.js by importing them directly in Node.js (they're plain JavaScript, no DOM needed).

Extract or export-wrap the pure functions for testing:
- escapeHtml
- escapeAttribute
- formatRelative
- formatVelocity
- formatEta
- describeError

### Tests
```
escapeHtml
  - '<script>' → '<script>'
  - '"' → '"'
  - "'" → '''
  - '&' → '&'
  - '>' → '>'
  - safe string unchanged

escapeAttribute (superset of escapeHtml + backtick)
  - backtick → '`'
  - inherits all escapeHtml cases

formatRelative (relative time strings)
  - 0ms ago → 'just now'
  - 30s ago → '30s ago'
  - 90s ago → '2m ago'
  - 3600s ago → '1h ago'
  - invalid ISO date → 'unknown'

formatVelocity
  - undefined/0 → '—'
  - 1.5 → '1.5/hr'
  - 0.1 → '0.1/hr'

formatEta
  - undefined → '—'
  - 0 → '—'
  - 0.5 (30 min) → '~30m'
  - 2.5 (2h30m) → '~2.5h'

describeError
  - Error instance → error.message
  - string → the string
  - null → 'Unknown error'
```

## Implementation note
Extract functions into a separate dashboard/utils.mjs module and import it, OR use a test shim that exports them from main.js.

title: Add FLYWHEEL_HOME env var to config loaders (needed by integration tests)
description:
## Why
Integration tests need to redirect ~/.flywheel to a temp directory so tests don't read or write the user's real config. Currently flywheelDir() is hardcoded to path.join(homedir(), '.flywheel').

## What
Update cli/config.ts flywheelDir() and cli/state.ts initDb() to respect:
```typescript
export function flywheelDir(): string {
  return process.env.FLYWHEEL_HOME ?? join(homedir(), '.flywheel');
}
```

Similarly, initDb() should use:
```typescript
const dbPath = process.env.FLYWHEEL_STATE_DB 
  ?? join(flywheelDir(), 'state.db');
```

## Acceptance
- `FLYWHEEL_HOME=/tmp/test-home flywheel doctor` reads config from /tmp/test-home/
- `FLYWHEEL_STATE_DB=:memory: flywheel runs` prints 'No runs yet' without touching ~/.flywheel/state.db
- Existing behavior (no env vars set) is unchanged
- Typechecks clean after change
- This bead must be completed before any integration test (cvw.3.*) can run

title: Epic: Integration Tests — SSH, remote runner, and NTM bridge
description:
Integration tests for the actual remote execution adapters: `SSHManager`, `RemoteCommandRunner`, and `NtmBridge`. These tests require a real configured VPS and real `ntm/br/bv` tooling, but still target the adapter layer directly rather than only the top-level CLI commands.

## Why this is missing today
Current E2E beads hit some remote behavior through `flywheel ssh test`, `preflight`, and lifecycle commands, but not the adapter APIs themselves. That leaves timeout mapping, stderr/exit-code translation, JSON parse contracts, and streaming behavior under-tested.

## Constraint
No mocks or fake SSH servers. Skip automatically when `~/.flywheel/ssh.yaml` is absent or the VPS cannot be reached.

title: test/integration/ssh-manager.e2e.ts — connect, exec, stream, timeout
description:

Scenarios:

- `exec()` with failing command returns non-zero code with stderr

Logging:

Acceptance:

title: Epic: SQLite State Layer + Config System
description:
The local state layer that persists all flywheel run data, events, and config. Uses SQLite (better-sqlite3, synchronous) for zero-async-complexity local storage. Combined with YAML config files for SSH, providers, and prompts.

## Why SQLite
- Local-only tool, no network DB needed
- Synchronous API (better-sqlite3) eliminates async complexity
- Single file, easy to backup/inspect
- Prepared statements for safety
- No ORM — raw SQL with type-safe wrappers

## 7-Table Schema (from pitch)
1. wizard_runs — planning sessions
2. flywheel_runs — per-project phase tracking + checkpoint SHA
3. ssh_connections — latency tracking + audit
4. prompt_sends — prompt injection audit trail
5. phase_events — append-only event log (THE source of truth for debugging/replay)
6. bead_snapshots — periodic bead state for velocity calc + time-travel
7. api_calls — per-API-call cost log for budget tracking

## Config Files
- ssh.yaml — VPS connection (created by SSH layer)
- providers.yaml — model keys + CAAM slots + pricing (chmod 600, never committed)
- prompts.yaml — 25+ prompts with model/effort/phase metadata
- config.toml — non-secret project state
